metadata.title: Arrow's Impossibility — Same Ballots, Different Winners

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Jambo, Liam — in for Bear. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart? Can you explain it, Bear?

props.command: Build it with a CLI, then take it apart?

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

beats.role_note: the problem, the context, the stakes — BEFORE the CLI
beats.narration_text: In 1951 Kenneth Arrow proved that no voting system can simultaneously satisfy four basic fairness criteria — it's mathematically impossible. The result is not a political opinion; it's a theorem. And the simplest way to feel it is to run the same one hundred ballots through four different systems and watch them disagree on who won.
beats.visual_intent: title card: 'Four voting methods, one set of ballots, four different winners — Arrow proved this is unavoidable'; no terminal yet

beats.narration_text: Ask Claude to run the election. Generate an election with a hundred voters in three groups — thirty-five prefer A over C over B, thirty-three prefer B over C over A, thirty-two prefer C over B over A. Compute the winner under Plurality, Borda Count, Instant Runoff, and Condorcet. Print a table: method, winner, votes or points, and which Arrow criterion is violated.

props.command: claude "Write a Python script: generate election with 100 voters in three groups (35: A>C>B, 33: B>C>A, 32: C>B>A). Compute winner under: Plurality, Borda Count (3pts/2pts/1pt), Instant-Runoff, Condorcet (pairwise majority). Print table: Method | Winner | Votes/Points | Arrow Criterion Violated."

beats.narration_text: Read the Borda count line — borda underscore scores is a dict mapping each candidate to the sum of group-size times three-minus-rank across all groups. The ranking system that seems fairest actually gives a different winner than every other method. The code is simple; the disagreement is not.

props.code:
# arrow_election.py  —  Claude Code output
groups = [(35,'ACB'),(33,'BCA'),(32,'CBA')]

# Plurality: most first-place votes
plurality = {c: sum(s for s,p in groups if p[0]==c)
             for c in 'ABC'}

# Borda: 3pts for 1st, 2pts for 2nd, 1pt for 3rd
borda = {c: sum(s*(3-p.index(c))
              for s,p in groups) for c in 'ABC'}
# Result: A=202, B=198, C=232 -> C wins Borda!

# IRV: eliminate lowest, redistribute
# C(32) eliminated, goes to B -> B wins 65 vs 35

# Condorcet: pairwise — B beats both A(65>35) and C

beats.narration_text: Four boxes, same hundred ballots. Plurality picks A — the most first choices. Borda picks C — the best average rank. Instant Runoff picks B — after eliminating the weakest. Condorcet picks B — beats everyone head to head. Arrow's theorem is not a thought experiment; it's the output of your own script.
beats.visual_intent: Manim: four voting method boxes (2x2 grid) each showing method, winner letter in color, violation text; ballot summary below

beats.narration_text: Now test the Independence of Irrelevant Alternatives criterion. Iterate: update the script — remove candidate C entirely and re-run Plurality and Borda. If the winner changes between A and B, IIA is violated. Print before-and-after comparison.

props.command: claude "update arrow_election.py: remove candidate C and re-run Plurality and Borda on the same 100 voters (re-express their A vs B preference). Print: winner with C present, winner without C, IIA violated yes/no."

beats.role_note: slate — fill with screen recording or Manim
beats.narration_text: With C on the ballot Borda picks C. Remove C and now B wins. An irrelevant candidate — one who cannot win — changed the outcome. That is the IIA failure, printed in your terminal.
beats.visual_intent: Comparison table: Borda winner WITH C vs WITHOUT C — showing IIA failure

beats.narration_text: Arrow's theorem says this is not a flaw in these four methods — it's a mathematical impossibility for any method. You cannot design a voting system that satisfies all four fairness criteria simultaneously. The best you can do is choose which criterion to sacrifice.
beats.visual_intent: recap card: Arrow's four criteria listed, each checked or crossed for each method; 'No system passes all four'

beats.narration_text: Your move: pick a real election — a ranked-choice ballot from a recent IRV election — and run it through all four methods. See which criterion your jurisdiction is implicitly sacrificing, and whether they know it.
beats.visual_intent: next-steps card: 1) find a real ranked-choice election dataset, 2) run all four methods, 3) identify violated criterion

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Here's what the body just demonstrated. Arrow's theorem says this is not a flaw in these four methods — it's a mathematical impossibility for any method. You cannot design a voting system that satisfies all four fairness criteria simultaneously. The best you can do is choose which criterion to sacrifice. Your move: pick a real election — a ranked-choice ballot from a recent IRV election — and run it through all four methods. See which criterion your jurisdiction is implicitly sacrificing, and whether they know it.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Arrow's Impossibility — Same Ballots, Different Winners] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
